item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoops, I'm crying.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me feel all warm and fuzzy inside. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Proposal and reasoning
Hide Proposal and reasoning

The purpose of the following edits is to connect this particular article to two other articles for which it's subject matter makes reference, specifically in regards to defunct Foundation properties, I.E. anomalies that have been neutralized.

Editing this:

As of 09/18/1997, six defunct Foundation properties and 3 defunct buildings belonging to Foundation fronts have been reported, although no active facilities have been listed.

To this:

As of 09/18/1997, six defunct Foundation properties[[footnote]]Including two neutralized anomalies, [[[SCP-1983]]] and [[[SCP-1522]]].[[/footnote]] and 3 defunct buildings belonging to Foundation fronts have been reported, although no active facilities have been listed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A user-adapting bucket list that demonstrates all the shortfalls of the type of books it looks like?

I feel that complaints about the item's "manufactured" nature are an interesting case of Poe's Law.

Upvoted, even though I don't like this sort of book myself, and it would be better to elaborate why some of the dreams generated by the item are mentioned to be nightmares?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same guy yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That final note is amazing, but the rest of the article really doesn't feel as good as it could have been. The suggested activities in particular read to me as something that's needlessly obfuscated.

+0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvoted because it left me with weird echoey undefined feelings i don't understand

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to give this a no-vote, because the listings really don't resonate with me at all. I know plenty of people who love that flavor of sentimentalism, but it's a little too strong for me. Then I realized that it would be perfect for the right reader. I'm upvoting, but I think this could be improved by having more listings and dividing them by reader. With more variety, you could grab everybody's heartstrings.

After reading Scantron's comment (the 2nd one), I wonder if facing one's mortality changes us as observers of nostalgia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By the way, is "that Gillespie boy" related in any way to the Gillespie mentioned in Skipping Time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came here for the same reason and GAH! Right in the nostalgia!

item.title: i feel ya.
item.content:encoded: why you make me cry! take my tear stained +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't read this fully, but I have to find it slightly amusing, because I came here because of one of Eric_H's stories, written almost exactly a year before this entry, which referenced someone discovering SCP-1841 in a comic book and commenting that they contained it last year… and this isn't exactly what you'd expect to be in a comic book. Going to read it later, but had to comment on this, because it amused me.

Coming back to this ~5 months later, this just feels really bland. The feels feel… manufactured. The phrasing and sentiments come off as artificial and unoriginal, even if the item itself is reasonably new in concept. Changing lack-of-vote into a downvote.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Huzzah, a hundred upvotes! Thank you everyone who read and enjoyed this!

Others before me have summed up the value within this article more thoroughly than I could. All I can do is reiterate that the deepness of emotion that the skilled words here writ have evoked is not often matched. That such was achieved within the constraints of the given format is impressive, and more than worthy of the meager vote I have to offer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why did I not see this before?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Roget, this is awesome. Extremely evocative without being cheesy, and quite understated in terms of its anomalous effect. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
someone remembered a thing i wrote :O

This is beautiful, Roget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking that too!
Well written. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. And it kinda reminds me of that movie Up. At least the beginning of it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Usually you guys tell the people to stop roleplaying and now …
(Late reply because of technical difficulties)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that it wasn't intentional somehow makes it even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Wow. I've actually never played a silent hill game past the first 2, and none of those were intentional references. That is one hell of a coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article. It's a simple idea, but really well written.

There's still something I have to point out. Maybe I'm wrong, and maybe it's not even done on purpose, but the five examples of additional entries really remind me of the five first Silent Hill games.

Dad's gone for a while now. Most of its been tore down or taken by that Gillespie boy,

The first game is the story of a father looking for his daughter after a car accident. The vilain is named Dahlia Gillespie.

The tall place - It's a forest somewhere. Remember, it was like what Mary wanted? You sat home, drawing those pictures for hours.

The second game is the story of a man looking for his dead wife, who's presumebly waiting for him in their "special place". The wife is called Mary.

Please be careful about the glass when you visit the old hiding spot, there's some loose shards there.

Heather, the protagonist of the third game, is afraid of mirrors. There's a part where a mirror almost kills you.

Outside - You can do it. It's been a long time, since you got to feel the dirt and mud in between the toes, but there is still an outside.

The protagonist of the fourth game is imprisoned in his own appartment. He's spending the rest of the game trying to get out, trying to escape the pocket dimension of a serial killer.

Travis' place - It took a long time, but he let it go. They all will, eventually.

Travis Grady, the protagonist of Origins (the fifth game), deals with his own traumatic past in that game, and learns to live with it.

If it's made on purpose, it's discrete enough and it's a nice touch. If it isn't, then this is the best coincidence I've ever seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So remind me, what does the C stand for again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The character, Nobody. or someone else.
If i missed some bigass-evil monsterdevilclef-thing nevermind but if this is safe in all ways of the word, i think this should not be contained.
i know they would never but you can ask

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I never recall them damn numbers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. There's a number of articles that do lists better (that AWCY app, and there was something written by Bright that had to do with Tamlin house i think) and the note doesn't affect me particularly either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck you, Roget. Hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason I was sure Flameshirt wrote this. I was expecting Pangloss to turn up.
+1 anyhow

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I think I might give it a go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always worth it to try, bro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Legit stuff man.

It would be nice to see a tale about a D class and his/her point of view. Perhaps they get assigned some research with this and then later on have a dream about an old place in their life. I would write it, but I'm not too confident when it comes to my writing skills.

Upvote for the memories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awww, okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. I forgot I posted here.

I'm gonna stick with my new post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well this is awkward

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. I feel like this might have more impact if I didn't already put a lot of thought into my mortality and what I want to do before death. Felt like a retread of my internal monologue. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Profound and heartwarming without the usual "OKAY NOW YOU FEEL THINGS" sappiness that tends to come with 'heartwarming' skips. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think… yeah, I think I'm going to turn off my computer, go out, and do something with my friends tonight.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It started out kinda iffy, but you had my emotions in a chokehold by the end of it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aagh! Suckerpunch right in the feels.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of my what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The entries gave me real actual goosebumps. Outstanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
please make nobody steal this and make him set this free.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ouch. That hurt the heartstrings.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still kind of meh on the concept, but this is much better executed than the "bucket list" version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very simple concept done extremely well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is lovely and bittersweet. I say this a lot, but in reminds in the best way of Stephen King. Its an entire story in one page.
Also reminds me of the Kick the Can sequence in the Twilight Zone movie.

touching. an invitation to live better. gather ye rosebuds while ye may
=D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one a lot. It works precisely because it's not another thing that's out for your blood. The writing on the back cover really speaks to me, as well. It's like SCP-1230, but instead of creating an adventure attuned to you in your dreams, it encourages you to find one in real life. That's very poetic and touching, in my opinion.

You just got a well deserved +1, Roget. Keep up the excellent work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Count the seeds in a pod. Maybe name them, so they don't grow up without anything to be called.

See all the shapes the clouds can make. Name the ones that they can't.

Yeah, I think I'm done for tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last addendum is really, really good, but the rest of the article doesn't support it. Maybe I'm just tired, but the excerpts don't do anything for me, and seem awfully short for a book of this format. The effect itself seems bland, and not especially anomalous. Neutral for now, but if you could streamline or spruce up the rest of the article, a hearty upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sir, you made me smile and maybe sad.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty solid stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I need to go cuddle up with my stuffed bamboo shark and a bag of goldfish crackers. This is brilliant.

item.title: Touching
item.content:encoded:
Great stuff. Evocative without being precious.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The feels, man.

+1

item.title: One way boat to feelstown.
item.content:encoded:
I will find you and kill you. I mean it in the best way.

+1
